onboarding.title: Joining the Lab
onboarding.body: New students begin with a staged onboarding period covering greenhouse safety, sample labeling, notebook standards, and instrument booking practices. During the first month, each student completes a small calibration exercise and a reproducible analysis walkthrough.

expectations: Arrive at meetings with a written experimental update, not only verbal impressions.
Record deviations from protocol immediately and preserve the original data trail.
Plan sampling logistics before treatment initiation, especially for time-sensitive assays.
Treat shared facilities and communal datasets as collective scientific infrastructure.
dataManagement: Every experiment receives a unique project code and dated folder structure before data collection begins.
Raw data are never overwritten. Cleaned data and derived tables must be versioned separately.
Metadata files should explain treatment factors, unit conventions, missing values, and instrument settings.
Figures intended for manuscripts should be reproducible from script or clearly documented spreadsheet steps.
reproducibility: Use pre-registered analysis notes for complex experiments where practical.
Retain randomization files, tray maps, and chamber placement logs.
Store calibration records alongside data summaries for analytical workflows such as HPLC.
Report exclusions explicitly and distinguish exploratory analyses from confirmatory comparisons.
software: R and RStudio for statistical analysis, ANOVA, and mixed-model workflows
Quarto for reproducible reports and internal method notes
Git and GitHub for version control of code, manuscripts, and templates
ImageJ and Python notebooks for phenotyping QC and batch processing
Zotero for reference management and reading group organization

faq.question: What makes a strong prospective student inquiry?
faq.answer: A concise message that explains your interests, prior methods experience, and why the lab’s current themes are relevant to your training goals.

faq.question: Do students need prior plant physiology experience?
faq.answer: Not always. We value quantitative curiosity, careful record keeping, and a willingness to learn methods in a disciplined way.

faq.question: How are projects structured?
faq.answer: Most projects combine one methods-focused training phase, one main experiment with clear design logic, and a reproducible analysis and writing phase.

faq.question: Can students work across greenhouse and field systems?
faq.answer: Yes. Several projects intentionally connect controlled-environment calibration with field validation when the research question requires it.
